---
title: "Validation map — from a single Ca²⁺ number to a portfolio of targets"
author: "Steve Haigh (with Claude Code)"
date: "2026-06-19"
format:
  pdf:
    include-in-header: ../pandoc-header.tex
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
---

# Why this doc

The "Dolan 5/5" criteria (Dolan & Diamond 2014, Fig. 4 — the cytosolic Ca²⁺
transient with and without extracellular Ca²⁺) were the model's headline
validation when the model *was* the Ca²⁺ transient. The model has since grown
into a multi-subsystem whole-cell model: the PI cycle, the GPCR cascade
(P2Y1/PAR1/PAR4/P2X1/TP), PKC feedback, granule secretion, thromboxane, integrin
(αIIbβ3), and now the inhibitory axis (P2Y12/Gi/cAMP/PKA/VASP). Validating that
whole model against one Ca²⁺ figure no longer reflects what the model does.

This doc re-frames the validation strategy: **keep Dolan 5/5 as a regression
invariant on the Ca²⁺ core, and judge the model on a portfolio of
subsystem-specific targets, each tied to a real experimental assay.**

# Why "5/5" is no longer the headline

Three reasons, each established from the current model:

1. **It passes by construction.** Every layer added since the Ca²⁺ core is
   deliberately normalised at rest so the resting fixed point and the Dolan
   transient are unchanged (e.g. the v0.7 PKA brake is exactly 1.0 at resting
   cAMP). A criterion engineered to keep passing is a *guard against accidental
   breakage*, not evidence that the new biology is correct.

2. **Low discriminating power (the Ca²⁺ clamp).** Cytosolic Ca²⁺ is
   store-limited and SOCE-clamped: it is nearly insensitive to any single
   release/clearance lever. Probes (2026-06-19) moved the peak by:

   | Lever | Range tested | Peak cyt Ca²⁺ effect |
   |---|---|---|
   | IP₃R open-probability brake | gain 1 → 8 | 293 → 298 nM (+5) |
   | PMCA $k_\mathrm{cat}$ | 1.0 → 0.3× | 408 → 412 nM (+4) |
   | SERCA forward rate | 1.0 → 0.3× | ~0 nM |

   The buffers (CaM / gelsolin / calreticulin) plus the SOCE↔clearance balance
   pin the cytosolic level. Many different parameterisations therefore pass
   Dolan 5/5 — it cannot discriminate between them.

3. **Scope mismatch.** The interesting, clinically-relevant biology has its own,
   stronger validation targets that the Ca²⁺ transient cannot speak to: the
   VASP/PRI assay for P2Y12 inhibition, PAC-1 flow cytometry for integrin
   activation, lumi-aggregometry for dense-granule release, and antiplatelet
   drug dose-response.

# The portfolio

Each subsystem is judged against the assay a wet lab would use. Status: ✓ has a
working comparison; ◐ partial / qualitative; ○ aspirational.

| Subsystem | Validation target (assay) | Status | Where |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ca²⁺ core | Dolan & Diamond 2014 Fig. 4 transient (±Ca_ex) | ✓ (now a **regression invariant**) | `test_phase3.py`, `phase3_dolan_fig4.py` |
| PI cycle / IP₃ | Resting IP₃ ≈ 50 nM; transient shape (Purvis 2008; Dolan Fig. S2) | ✓ | `test_regression.py` |
| PKC feedback | "Rapid, substantial" P2Y1 desensitisation; IP₃ ↓ (Mundell 2006; Purvis 2008) | ◐ direction + magnitude band | `test_validation_targets.py` |
| Granule secretion | Lumi-aggregometry (ATP/5-HT release kinetics); P-selectin flow | ◐ sigmoidal release shown | `granule_secretion.py` plot |
| Thromboxane | Aspirin abolishes TXA₂; autocrine second wave | ✓ direction | `test_thromboxane.py`, second-wave tests |
| Integrin (αIIbβ3) | PAC-1 flow cytometry; graded dose-response; drug shifts | ✓ new (this work) | `plotInhibitoryAxis.py`, `test_integrin.py` |
| Inhibitory axis | VASP/PRI assay (P2Y12 inhibition); PGI₂/forskolin Ca²⁺ suppression | ✓ VASP/PRI direction; ○ PGI₂ (future slice) | `test_inhibitory_axis.py`, `plotInhibitoryAxis.py` |

The v0.7 inhibitory-axis work added two genuine portfolio targets — the VASP/PRI
readout and a PAC-1 drug-response — neither of which the Ca²⁺ transient could
provide.

# The Ca²⁺ clamp as a stated limitation

The clamp is not a bug to hide; it is a model property with consequences worth
stating in the thesis:

- **What it means.** The model currently cannot quantitatively predict changes in
  cytosolic Ca²⁺ *amplitude* driven by upstream modulation (PKC, P2Y12/cAMP).
  Those signals are real in the model but are absorbed by buffering and the SOCE
  clamp before they reach free cytosolic Ca²⁺.

- **How we work with it.** Feedback and inhibitory signals are validated and made
  visible on the readouts they actually move — the functional outputs (integrin,
  secretion, thromboxane) and the signalling-node readouts (IP₃, cAMP, VASP-P) —
  rather than on free cytosolic Ca²⁺. This is why the v0.7 PKA brake acts on the
  αIIbβ3 inside-out rate (visible: clopidogrel lowers PAC-1) as well as on IP₃R
  (real but ~invisible on cyt Ca²⁺).

- **Future work.** Revisiting the cytosolic buffer load and the SOCE-clamp
  calibration so that upstream modulation reaches free Ca²⁺ is a candidate
  Ca²⁺-core project. It would need its own re-validation against Dolan — which is
  exactly the regression-invariant role 5/5 is well suited to.

# Recommendation

1. Keep Dolan 5/5 as the cheap regression invariant on the Ca²⁺ core (done — see
   the re-worded `test_phase3.py` docstring).
2. Present the model's validation as the portfolio table above; report each
   subsystem against its own assay.
3. State the Ca²⁺ clamp as a known limitation with the future-work hook.

# References

*Verify in Zotero before citing (titles / first authors / approximate years are
safe-to-recall anchors only).*

- **Dolan & Diamond 2014** *Biophys. J.* 106:2049 — Ca²⁺ transient validation.
- **Purvis et al. 2008** *Blood* 112:4069 — PI/Ca model; resting IP₃.
- **Mundell et al. 2006** — PKC-dependent P2Y1 desensitisation.
- **Gachet 2012** *Purinergic Signal.* 8:609 — P2Y12 / Gi; the basis for the
  VASP/PRI readout direction.
- **Burkhart et al. 2012** *Blood* 120:e73 — platelet proteome copy numbers.
